summary: Gruber celebrates the resurrection of the PGP product line under the new PGP Corporation, after Network Associates bungled its stewardship. The key news is that PGP Desktop Security for Mac OS X will ship before end of 2002, with the software already completed but previously shelved. Gruber uses this as a springboard to argue that open source alternatives like GPG, while cryptographically sound, fail at user interface design. He broadens this into a critique of the Unix-world misconception that GUIs are superficial wrappers around 'real' software, citing Sun's StarOffice as another example of confusing visual polish with genuine usability. The core argument is that good UI is deep engineering work, not a cosmetic layer.
key_points: PGP Corporation rescued the PGP product line from Network Associates, who had killed it despite completed Mac OS X and Windows XP versions sitting ready to ship
GPG is cryptographically equivalent to PGP but fails as end-user software because good UI design is genuinely hard engineering work, not a superficial addition
The Unix world harbors a widespread misconception that a GUI is a thin skin over 'real' software, when in fact the difference between good and bad UI runs deep
Sun's StarOffice pitch to Apple reveals the same misunderstanding — thinking Mac users reject software for being ugly rather than for being unintuitive and non-conformant to platform conventions
money_quotes: What's wrong with GPG is what's wrong with so much open source software — the user interface.
Open source software has proven itself to be fantastic for creating developer-level technology, and horrible at creating end-user software.
User interface design is hard work, and implementing a good design takes a long time.

There is a widespread misconception in the Unix world that a GUI is something that sits on top of 'real' software; that all the real engineering work is done at low levels, producing APIs and command-line tools, and that one can reasonably expect to wrap a GUI around the completed low level technologies in short order.
Sure, *that's* the problem: the current StarOffice interface isn't 'beautiful'. It doesn't have anything to do with being unintuitive, unwieldy, and not conforming to any standard Mac UI guidelines.
Sun thinks that the only thing StarOffice needs to achieve popularity on Mac OS X is better graphic design.
Nobody makes a product more beautiful on Apple than Apple.
key_insight: Good user interface design is deep engineering work, not a cosmetic layer, and this fundamental misunderstanding is why open source software and companies like Sun consistently fail to create compelling end-user experiences.
